page: Google Cloud Platform c4n-highcpu-96 is a compute-optimized virtual machine instance belonging to the c4n server family. It is built on the x86_64 CPU architecture and features 96 dedicated virtual central processing units (vCPUs). The instance is provisioned with 192.0 GB of system memory, yielding a resource density of 2.0 GB of RAM per vCPU core.

This server configuration does not include bundled local NVMe storage or integrated graphics processing units (GPUs). Network and storage traffic rely entirely on external network-attached block storage options. Additionally, the instance does not include complimentary public IPv4 addresses, meaning external connectivity must be configured via standard cloud networking mechanisms.

With a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio, the c4n-highcpu-96 is designed for compute-heavy workloads that require substantial parallel processing power without massive memory footprints. This hardware profile is suited for high-performance computing (HPC), batch processing, intensive web serving, and distributed analytics. From a qualitative cost-efficiency perspective, the dedicated CPU allocation and high core density provide a predictable compute environment, minimizing resource contention for CPU-bound tasks.
description: Google Cloud Platform c4n-highcpu-96 is an x86_64 architecture virtual machine instance within the c4n family, designed for compute-intensive workloads. It features 96 dedicated vCPUs and 192.0 GB of system memory, establishing a 1:2 vCPU-to-RAM ratio with 2.0 GB of memory per core. This instance does not include local storage or integrated GPUs, relying on external network-attached storage for data persistence. It also does not provide complimentary public IPv4 addresses. The high density of dedicated vCPUs makes this server configuration suitable for parallel processing tasks, high-performance computing, batch processing, and compute-heavy web serving. The dedicated CPU allocation ensures consistent performance for workloads that require sustained processing power without the overhead of massive memory allocations.
